<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Intersection theory chow</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>

<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

Intersection theory chow

Algebraic cycles modulo rational equivalence: Chow rings, Chern classes, and Bezout generalized.

blocks.html:
1. Algebraic cycles and rational equivalence

Fix a variety (or scheme) $X$ over a field $k$. The group of algebraic $k$-cycles $Z_k(X)$ is the free abelian group on closed irreducible subvarieties of dimension $k$. A cycle $\alpha \in Z_k(X)$ is therefore a finite formal sum $\sum n_i [V_i]$ with $n_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ and each $V_i \subseteq X$ a closed subvariety of dimension exactly $k$. Note the dimension grading is by algebraic dimension; the codimension grading $Z^c(X) = Z_{\dim X - c}(X)$ is the one used when $X$ is equidimensional.

Naked cycles are too rigid: $[\{0\}]$ and $[\{1\}]$ are different elements of $Z_0(\mathbb{A}^1)$, even though every reasonable invariant should regard them as the "same" $0$-cycle. The remedy is rational equivalence. Two $k$-cycles $\alpha, \alpha' \in Z_k(X)$ are rationally equivalent, $\alpha \sim_{\mathrm{rat}} \alpha'$, if there exist $(k+1)$-dimensional subvarieties $W_j \subseteq X$ and rational functions $f_j \in k(W_j)^\times$ such that

$$ \alpha - \alpha' \;=\; \sum_j \mathrm{div}_{W_j}(f_j) \;\in\; Z_k(X). $$

Each $\mathrm{div}_{W_j}(f_j)$ is the principal Weil divisor on $W_j$ pushed forward to $X$ — a $\mathbb{Z}$-linear combination of the codimension-one subvarieties of $W_j$, weighted by the order of vanishing or pole of $f_j$ along each. The slogan is: rational equivalence is the connecting homotopy in the world of algebraic cycles, with $\mathbb{P}^1$ playing the role of the unit interval. Indeed, $\alpha \sim_{\mathrm{rat}} \alpha'$ iff there is a cycle $\Gamma$ on $X \times \mathbb{P}^1$ (flat over $\mathbb{P}^1$ on each component) whose fibre over $0$ is $\alpha$ and whose fibre over $\infty$ is $\alpha'$.

Concretely on $\mathbb{P}^1$ itself: any two distinct points are rationally equivalent, because the rational function $f(t) = t - a$ on $\mathbb{P}^1$ has $\mathrm{div}(f) = [a] - [\infty]$ — proving $[a] \sim [\infty]$ for every $a \in \mathbb{A}^1$, hence all $0$-cycles of degree $1$ on $\mathbb{P}^1$ collapse to a single class. The widget below walks through the core example: a one-parameter family $t \mapsto [1 : t]$ that degenerates two distinct points on $\mathbb{P}^1$ into a single doubled point, exhibiting the rational equivalence as a divisor on a $1$-cycle (the family graph) sitting inside $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$. Compare this with $\bezout$'s degree count and with the cycle classes that show up as fundamental classes in $\schemes$ — rational equivalence is what turns subvarieties into "homology-like" classes.

  • Closed subschemes, reducedness, and irreducibility

Used in
Intersection theory and Chow groups · Chow groups $A_*(X)$

2. Chow groups $A_*(X)$

Quotienting out by rational equivalence packages every cycle on $X$ into a graded abelian group:

$$ A_k(X) \;=\; Z_k(X) \big/ \mathrm{Rat}_k(X), \qquad A_*(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{k \ge 0} A_k(X). $$

For smooth $X$ of pure dimension $n$ we re-grade by codimension $A^c(X) = A_{n-c}(X)$ and write $A^*(X) = \bigoplus_c A^c(X)$. The intersection product (next section) makes $A^*(X)$ a graded ring; without smoothness one only has $A_*$ as a covariantly-functorial graded group with a cap-product action.

Three computations to internalise. First: $A_*(\mathbb{A}^n) = A_n(\mathbb{A}^n) \cdot [\mathbb{A}^n] \cong \mathbb{Z}$ — every cycle of dimension $< n$ is rationally equivalent to $0$ via translation in the family $\mathbb{A}^n \times \mathbb{A}^1 \to \mathbb{A}^n$ (push everything to infinity). Second: $A_*(\mathbb{P}^n) \cong \mathbb{Z}^{n+1}$, generated by the class $[L^k]$ of a linear subspace of dimension $k$ for each $0 \le k \le n$. Third (functoriality): a proper morphism $f\colon X \to Y$ induces a covariant push-forward $f_*\colon A_k(X) \to A_k(Y)$ via $f_*[V] = \deg(V/f(V)) \cdot [f(V)]$ (with $\deg = 0$ when $V \to f(V)$ is not generically finite); a flat morphism induces a contravariant pull-back $f^*\colon A_k(Y) \to A_{k+r}(X)$ where $r$ is the relative dimension.

Conceptually, $A_*$ is the algebraic analogue of singular homology: there is a cycle map $\mathrm{cl}\colon A_*(X) \to H_{2*}^{\mathrm{BM}}(X(\mathbb{C}); \mathbb{Z})$ to Borel–Moore homology when $X$ is over $\mathbb{C}$, but it is generally far from injective or surjective — the kernel detects "algebraic but homologically trivial" cycles (Griffiths' results on the Fano threefold), and the cokernel reflects transcendental classes invisible to algebraic geometry. The widget below lets you click on a cycle in a small toy ground set (degree-$d$ effective $0$-cycles on a genus-$1$ curve) and see which other cycles share its rational-equivalence class.

  • Schemes — every closed subvariety appearing here is the support of a closed subscheme; rational equivalence is most cleanly stated in scheme-theoretic language.

Used in
Intersection theory and Chow groups · Intersection product on smooth $X$

3. Intersection product on smooth $X$

For a smooth variety $X$ of dimension $n$, the Chow group $A^*(X)$ carries a graded-commutative intersection product

$$ \cdot \;:\; A^p(X) \otimes A^q(X) \longrightarrow A^{p+q}(X) $$

characterised on transverse pairs by counting set-theoretic intersection points with multiplicity. Concretely, if $Y \subset X$ is a subvariety of codimension $p$ and $Z \subset X$ is of codimension $q$, and if $Y, Z$ meet transversally (every component of $Y \cap Z$ has the expected codimension $p + q$ and the tangent spaces span $T_x X$ at every smooth point of intersection), then $$ [Y] \cdot [Z] \;=\; [Y \cap Z] \;\in\; A^{p+q}(X). $$ When $p + q = n$ the right-hand side is a $0$-cycle, and one gets a numerical intersection number $\#(Y \cdot Z) \in \mathbb{Z}$ by passing to degree (i.e. push-forward to $\mathrm{Spec}\, k$).

The non-transverse case is the whole game. Two cycles need not meet transversally — even worse, $[Y] \cdot [Y]$ asks for the self-intersection, which is meaningless set-theoretically. The moving lemma (Chow, Roberts) rescues this on smooth quasi-projective $X$: every cycle class is rationally equivalent to one in general position with any fixed cycle, so we can replace one of $Y, Z$ by an equivalent cycle that does meet the other transversally and then count points. Well-definedness is the content of the moving-lemma proof. On singular $X$ the moving lemma fails in general — one has to descend to operational Chow groups or work with the bivariant theory of Fulton–MacPherson.

The local intersection multiplicity at an isolated component $W$ of $Y \cap Z$ is computed by Serre's formula $$ i(Y, Z; W) \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \, \mathrm{length}_{\mathcal{O}_{X,W}} \mathrm{Tor}^{\mathcal{O}_{X,W}}_i(\mathcal{O}_Y, \mathcal{O}_Z). $$ When $Y, Z$ are Cohen–Macaulay and meet in the expected codimension only the $i=0$ term survives, giving the geometric multiplicity $\mathrm{length}\, \mathcal{O}_{X,W}/(I_Y + I_Z)$. Click an intersection point in the surface widget to see how the contribution at that point assembles into the total $Y \cdot Z$.

Used in
Intersection theory and Chow groups · $A^*(\mathbb{P}^n) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^{n+1})$
Intersection theory and Chow groups · Chern classes

4. $A^*(\mathbb{P}^n) = \mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^{n+1})$

Among all smooth varieties, projective space has the simplest Chow ring imaginable: a truncated polynomial ring on a single generator. Let $h \in A^1(\mathbb{P}^n)$ denote the class of a hyperplane $H \subset \mathbb{P}^n$. Then

$$ A^*(\mathbb{P}^n) \;=\; \mathbb{Z}[h] \,\big/\, (h^{n+1}), \qquad A^k(\mathbb{P}^n) = \mathbb{Z} \cdot h^k \quad (0 \le k \le n). $$

The geometric content of $h^k$ is the class of any codimension-$k$ linear subspace $L^{n-k} \subset \mathbb{P}^n$ — every two such are rationally equivalent (move them via a linear family). The relation $h^{n+1} = 0$ is forced by dimension: a codimension-$(n+1)$ subvariety of $\mathbb{P}^n$ doesn't exist (it would have negative dimension), so its class is $0$.

The intersection product is multiplication of polynomials in $h$ followed by truncation. Two effective hypersurfaces of degrees $d_1, d_2$ have classes $d_1 h$ and $d_2 h$, so they intersect in the class $d_1 d_2 \, h^2$. For $n = 2$ this is exactly Bézout's theorem: $C \cdot D = (\deg C)(\deg D)$ points (with multiplicity) in $\mathbb{P}^2$. For general $n$, intersecting $n$ hypersurfaces of degrees $d_1, \ldots, d_n$ in general position gives $$ d_1 h \cdot d_2 h \cdots d_n h \;=\; (d_1 d_2 \cdots d_n) \, h^n \;\in\; A^n(\mathbb{P}^n) = \mathbb{Z}, $$ recovering the projective Bézout count. Higher products of the same generator pile up "schubert-like" classes: $h^k$ has degree $1$ as the class of a codimension-$k$ linear space, and the intersection $h^a \cdot h^b = h^{a+b}$ if $a + b \le n$, else $0$.

The proof uses the affine cell decomposition $\mathbb{P}^n = \mathbb{A}^n \sqcup \mathbb{A}^{n-1} \sqcup \cdots \sqcup \mathbb{A}^0$ (Schubert cells), Quillen's affine bundle lemma $A_*(\mathbb{A}^n) = \mathbb{Z}$, and the localization sequence to assemble $A^*(\mathbb{P}^n)$ from the cells. The widget below lets you pick $n$ and explore the resulting ring; it also lets you compute $h^a \cdot h^b$ for arbitrary $a, b$.

  • Projective points and lines

5. Chern classes

A rank-$r$ vector bundle $E$ on a smooth variety $X$ carries an axiomatically-defined sequence of Chern classes $c_i(E) \in A^i(X)$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, r$, packaged into the total Chern class $$ c(E) \;=\; 1 + c_1(E) + c_2(E) + \cdots + c_r(E) \;\in\; A^*(X). $$ Concretely, $c_i(E)$ is the class of the locus where $r - i + 1$ generic sections of $E$ become linearly dependent — the obstruction to extending $i$ chosen sections to a frame on a Zariski-open set.

The Grothendieck axioms pin them down uniquely:

  • Naturality. $c_i(f^*E) = f^* c_i(E)$ for any morphism $f\colon Y \to X$.
  • Whitney sum / multiplicativity. For every short exact sequence $0 \to E' \to E \to E'' \to 0$ of vector bundles on $X$, the total Chern classes multiply: $$ c(E) \;=\; c(E') \cdot c(E'') \;\in\; A^*(X). $$ Equivalently, in degrees: $c_k(E) = \sum_{i+j=k} c_i(E') \, c_j(E'')$.
  • Normalisation. For a line bundle $L$, $c(L) = 1 + c_1(L)$, where $c_1(L)$ is the class of the divisor of zeros of any rational section.

Multiplicativity is the lever that makes Chern classes computable. Given a filtration $0 = E_0 \subset E_1 \subset \cdots \subset E_r = E$ by sub-bundles with line-bundle quotients $L_i = E_i / E_{i-1}$, the total Chern class factors as $$ c(E) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^r (1 + \alpha_i), \qquad \alpha_i = c_1(L_i). $$ The classes $\alpha_i$ are the Chern roots of $E$. They live not in $A^*(X)$ but in the cohomology of an auxiliary "splitting principle" cover; the elementary symmetric functions of the $\alpha_i$ recover $c_i(E)$. Anything you want to compute about $E$ — Chern character, Todd class, top Chern class as Euler class — comes from these Chern roots.

The widget lets you build a rank-$r$ bundle as the direct sum of line bundles with chosen first Chern classes and watches $c(E)$ assemble multiplicatively. For non-split bundles the same arithmetic governs whatever filtration you can find.

Intersection theory and Chow groups · Grothendieck–Riemann–Roch (preview)

6. Grothendieck–Riemann–Roch (preview)

The classical Riemann–Roch theorem on a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g$ asserts that for any divisor $D$, $$ \chi(C, \mathcal{O}(D)) \;=\; \deg D + 1 - g. $$ For a smooth surface $S$ and an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}(D)$, the analogue is $$ \chi(S, \mathcal{O}(D)) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} D \cdot (D - K_S) + \chi(S, \mathcal{O}_S). $$ Each formula is an Euler-characteristic computation expressed in terms of intersection numbers and a discrete invariant of $X$ (genus, canonical class, Euler characteristic). Grothendieck–Riemann–Roch is the statement that uniformly produces every such formula from a single functorial equation.

The data: define the Chern character by $\mathrm{ch}(E) = \sum_i e^{\alpha_i} \in A^*(X) \otimes \mathbb{Q}$ where the $\alpha_i$ are the Chern roots; concretely $\mathrm{ch}(E) = \mathrm{rk}(E) + c_1 + \tfrac12(c_1^2 - 2c_2) + \cdots$. Define the Todd class by $\mathrm{td}(E) = \prod_i \tfrac{\alpha_i}{1 - e^{-\alpha_i}}$; its first few terms are $\mathrm{td}(E) = 1 + \tfrac12 c_1 + \tfrac1{12}(c_1^2 + c_2) + \cdots$. Both are multiplicative in short exact sequences (after suitable interpretation), and both extend to the Grothendieck group $K_0(X)$ — the abelian group generated by coherent sheaves modulo the relation $[\mathcal{F}] = [\mathcal{F}'] + [\mathcal{F}'']$ for short exact sequences.

The theorem: for a proper morphism $f\colon X \to Y$ of smooth varieties and a coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$, $$ \boxed{ \;\mathrm{ch}(f_* \mathcal{F}) \cdot \mathrm{td}(T_Y) \;=\; f_* \bigl(\mathrm{ch}(\mathcal{F}) \cdot \mathrm{td}(T_X)\bigr) \;\in\; A^*(Y) \otimes \mathbb{Q}. \;} $$ When $Y = \mathrm{Spec}\, k$, the left-hand side is just $\chi(X, \mathcal{F}) \cdot 1$ and the right-hand side is the Hirzebruch–Riemann–Roch formula $\int_X \mathrm{ch}(\mathcal{F}) \cdot \mathrm{td}(T_X)$. Specialising further to curves recovers the classical Riemann–Roch; specialising to surfaces gives Noether's formula and the surface Riemann–Roch above. The widget walks through the assembly of GRR on $X = \mathbb{P}^2$ for a line bundle $\mathcal{O}(d)$.

params.bodyScript:
<script>
(function(){
  if(!window.MVProofScrubber) return;
  MVProofScrubber.init('#w-grr-scrub', {
    title: "GRR on $\\mathbb{P}^2$ for $\\mathcal{O}(d)$",
    hint: "drag the slider — assemble the Hirzebruch RR formula step by step",
    viewBox: "0 0 360 200",
    autoplayMs: 2600,
    steps: [
      {
        title: "The data on $\\mathbb{P}^2$",
        body: "Take $X = \\mathbb{P}^2$, $\\mathcal{F} = \\mathcal{O}(d)$, and $f\\colon \\mathbb{P}^2 \\to \\mathrm{Spec}\\, k$. We want $\\chi(\\mathbb{P}^2, \\mathcal{O}(d))$. The Chow ring is $A^*(\\mathbb{P}^2) = \\mathbb{Z}[h]/(h^3)$ — $h$ the hyperplane class, $h^2$ the class of a point.",
        svgInner: "<rect x='30' y='40' width='300' height='110' rx='10' fill='var(--panel2)' stroke='var(--blue)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='180' y='75' text-anchor='middle' font-size='13' fill='var(--ink)'>X = ℙ², F = 𝒪(d), f: ℙ² → Spec k</text><text x='180' y='105' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11' fill='var(--mute)'>A*(ℙ²) = ℤ[h]/(h³)</text><text x='180' y='125' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11' fill='var(--mute)'>χ(ℙ², 𝒪(d)) = ?</text>"
      },
      {
        title: "Chern character $\\mathrm{ch}(\\mathcal{O}(d))$",
        body: "The first Chern class $c_1(\\mathcal{O}(d)) = d h$. As a line bundle there is one Chern root $\\alpha_1 = d h$. So $$ \\mathrm{ch}(\\mathcal{O}(d)) \\;=\\; e^{dh} \\;=\\; 1 + d h + \\tfrac{d^2}{2} h^2. $$ (Higher terms vanish in $A^*(\\mathbb{P}^2)$ because $h^3 = 0$.)",
        svgInner: "<text x='180' y='40' text-anchor='middle' font-size='13' fill='var(--cyan)'>ch(𝒪(d)) = e^{dh}</text><text x='180' y='80' text-anchor='middle' font-size='14' fill='var(--ink)'>= 1 + dh + (d²/2) h²</text><text x='180' y='130' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11' fill='var(--mute)'>(truncate at h³ = 0)</text>"
      },
      {
        title: "Todd class $\\mathrm{td}(T_{\\mathbb{P}^2})$",
        body: "From the Euler sequence $0 \\to \\mathcal{O} \\to \\mathcal{O}(1)^{\\oplus 3} \\to T_{\\mathbb{P}^2} \\to 0$ we get $c(T_{\\mathbb{P}^2}) = (1 + h)^3 = 1 + 3h + 3h^2$. Splitting into roots: $T_{\\mathbb{P}^2}$ has Chern roots $\\alpha_1, \\alpha_2$ with $\\alpha_1 + \\alpha_2 = 3h$ and $\\alpha_1 \\alpha_2 = 3h^2$. Then $$ \\mathrm{td}(T_{\\mathbb{P}^2}) = 1 + \\tfrac32 h + h^2. $$",
        svgInner: "<text x='180' y='40' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--ink)'>0 → 𝒪 → 𝒪(1)^3 → T_{ℙ²} → 0</text><text x='180' y='75' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--ink)'>c(T) = (1+h)³ = 1 + 3h + 3h²</text><text x='180' y='115' text-anchor='middle' font-size='13' fill='var(--violet)'>td(T) = 1 + (3/2)h + h²</text>"
      },
      {
        title: "Multiply: $\\mathrm{ch} \\cdot \\mathrm{td}$",
        body: "Multiply in $A^*(\\mathbb{P}^2) \\otimes \\mathbb{Q}$, dropping $h^k$ for $k \\ge 3$: $$ \\mathrm{ch}(\\mathcal{O}(d)) \\cdot \\mathrm{td}(T_{\\mathbb{P}^2}) \\;=\\; \\bigl(1 + dh + \\tfrac{d^2}{2} h^2\\bigr) \\bigl(1 + \\tfrac{3}{2} h + h^2\\bigr). $$ The coefficient of $h^2$ is $1 + \\tfrac{3d}{2} + \\tfrac{d^2}{2} = \\tfrac{(d+1)(d+2)}{2}$.",
        svgInner: "<text x='180' y='50' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--ink)'>(1 + dh + (d²/2)h²)·(1 + (3/2)h + h²)</text><text x='180' y='95' text-anchor='middle' font-size='13' fill='var(--yellow)'>[h²] = 1 + 3d/2 + d²/2</text><text x='180' y='125' text-anchor='middle' font-size='14' fill='var(--green)'>= (d+1)(d+2)/2</text>"
      },
      {
        title: "Push forward: $\\chi$ is the degree",
        body: "Because $f\\colon \\mathbb{P}^2 \\to \\mathrm{pt}$ has $f_*$ equal to the degree map on $A^*$, and only $h^2$ survives push-forward (it is the class of a point), $$ \\chi(\\mathbb{P}^2, \\mathcal{O}(d)) \\;=\\; f_*\\bigl(\\mathrm{ch}(\\mathcal{O}(d)) \\cdot \\mathrm{td}(T_{\\mathbb{P}^2})\\bigr) \\;=\\; \\tbinom{d+2}{2}. $$ The closed-form formula matches the elementary count $\\dim H^0(\\mathbb{P}^2, \\mathcal{O}(d)) = \\binom{d+2}{2}$ for $d \\ge 0$ (with higher cohomology vanishing).",
        svgInner: "<text x='180' y='40' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--ink)'>f_*: A*(ℙ²) → A*(pt) = ℤ</text><text x='180' y='75' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--mute)'>only [h²] = [pt] survives</text><rect x='80' y='100' width='200' height='40' rx='8' fill='var(--panel2)' stroke='var(--green)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='125' text-anchor='middle' font-size='14' fill='var(--green)' font-weight='600'>χ(ℙ², 𝒪(d)) = (d+1)(d+2)/2</text>"
      },
      {
        title: "Why this is the right packaging",
        body: "Replacing $\\mathbb{P}^2$ by a curve $C$ recovers $\\chi(C, \\mathcal{O}(D)) = \\deg D + 1 - g$ from $\\mathrm{ch}(\\mathcal{O}(D)) = 1 + D$ and $\\mathrm{td}(T_C) = 1 + \\tfrac12 c_1(T_C) = 1 - \\tfrac12 K_C$, with $\\deg(-\\tfrac12 K_C) = 1 - g$. Replacing it with a surface gives the Noether formula. The functoriality of GRR — pushing along any proper $f$ — is what lets you mix-and-match Riemann–Roch on different varieties without re-deriving anything.",
        svgInner: "<text x='180' y='30' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--mute)'>same formula, three specialisations:</text><text x='180' y='65' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--ink)'>curve C: χ = deg D + 1 − g</text><text x='180' y='95' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--ink)'>surface S: χ = D·(D−K)/2 + χ(𝒪)</text><text x='180' y='125' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--ink)'>ℙⁿ: χ(𝒪(d)) = (d+n)!/(d! n!)</text><text x='180' y='160' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12' fill='var(--yellow)' font-weight='600'>all from one GRR equation</text>"
      }
    ]
  });
})();
</script>
